title: St. Aristarchus, St. Pudens, St. Trophimus & The 42 Martyrs of Amorium
narrative:
St. Aristarchus of Thessalonica, St. Pudens the Senator, St. Trophimus the Apostle, and the Forty-Two Martyrs of Amorium

On the nineteenth of Megabit, the holy Church gathers the companions of the Apostle Paul into an assembly that illuminates the network of relationships through which the apostolic mission was sustained and the Gospel was carried from city to city across the Mediterranean world. Saint Aristarchus of Thessalonica was one of Paul's most faithful travelling companions — present at the riot in Ephesus, accompanying Paul on his final journey to Rome, sharing his imprisonment and described by the apostle as a fellow prisoner who was with him in the most demanding periods of his captivity. His faithfulness to Paul in prison is among the most touching details in the Pauline correspondence: not the dramatic missionary journey or the founding of churches but the willingness to stay beside a prisoner, to share his captivity, to be numbered among the prisoners, is the form of apostolic service that Aristarchus embodies.

Saint Pudens the Roman Senator is one of the most debated figures in the entire early hagiographical tradition: mentioned by Paul in his second letter to Timothy as one who sends greetings along with Claudia, Pudens is identified by some traditions as a Roman senator who opened his house as one of the earliest Christian meeting places in Rome, who may have hosted Peter himself, and from whose household the church of Santa Pudenziana in Rome claims its foundation. Whether or not all these identifications are historical, the figure of the Roman senator who put his aristocratic household at the service of the apostolic mission represents something real in the early church's social history: that Christianity found its early Roman footholds in households of some social standing, and that the willingness of such householders to use their privilege for the service of the apostles was itself a form of apostolic ministry.

Saint Trophimus the Apostle, a Gentile Christian of Ephesus and another of Paul's companions whose name appears at several points in the Acts and the Pauline letters, is the representative of the Gentile mission in this assembly: one of those who demonstrated in his own person that the Gospel had broken through the boundary of Jewish particularism and was producing genuine disciples in the Gentile world. He accompanied Paul on his last journey to Jerusalem, and his presence in the temple precinct was the occasion — or the pretext — for the riot that led to Paul's arrest. That a Gentile's presence in Paul's company was itself sufficient to provoke a violent reaction in Jerusalem tells us everything about the stakes of the apostolic mission.

The Forty-Two Martyrs of Amorium are the great medieval company of this day's assembly: Byzantine soldiers who were captured during the Arab sack of the Phrygian city of Amorium in eight hundred and thirty-eight and who were held captive in Samarra for seven years before being executed for refusing to convert to Islam. Their captivity was prolonged precisely because their captors hoped that time would achieve what threat and persuasion had not; their execution, after seven years, was the captors' acknowledgment that it had not. The Ethiopian tradition honours the Amorium martyrs as witnesses to the specific form of the Lenten virtue of perseverance — not the sudden courage of the execution morning but the sustained daily endurance of seven years of imprisonment in a foreign land, sustained by prayer and mutual encouragement, waiting for an end that came not as release but as martyrdom. Amen.
